docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
This manual documents the current equipment, configurations, and handling practices used in the aha-astrolab astronomy setup.

Its purpose is to provide clear, step-by-step guidance for safely and effectively operating the instruments, with a focus on solar observation and light visual use at night. It covers mechanical setup, component usage, practical tips, and workflow habits based on direct experience.

This manual will not teach amateur astronomy, but it is intended to support such endeavors by making the equipment easier to use and reducing friction during sessions.

Reading it is strongly recommended before using the system — unless, of course, you are already a very experienced telescope handler.

The manual does not include theoretical background, observing techniques, or astrophotography processing instructions. These are considered out of scope, though occasional context may be mentioned where relevant to equipment handling.

All procedures reflect the present state of the equipment. Future upgrades or reconfigurations may be documented separately or as additions to this manual.

Instruments and equipments of the aha-astrolab in use (imagining the sun in h-alpha light)

docs.title: Camera and Adapter Overview
docs.text:
The ASI174MM comes with a T2-to-1.25" adapter, allowing the camera to be mounted like a standard 1.25" eyepiece.

While this is useful in some configurations, it is recommended to use the T2 thread connection whenever possible. Threaded connections are mechanically more stable, reduce potential for tilt, and allow for more secure long-term mounting during imaging.

When attaching or removing the camera from a T2 connection, always hold the black base section of the camera. Gripping only the red housing may cause the body to unscrew from its base, exposing the sensor and internal electronics.

When using the T2 connection, it is best to connect the USB cable after the camera is fully mounted. However, when using the 1.25" eyepiece adapter, connecting the USB cable before insertion can help gravity rotate the camera into a natural and stable orientation.

This manual favors T2 mounting as the default unless specific hardware requires a 1.25" interface.

The camera (Red) and its T2-1.25" adapter (black) The camera (red) with the T2-1.25" adapter (black) mounted: Now can be mounted to any 1.25" eyepiece holder

docs.title: Configuring the Powermate 2x for T2 Mounting
docs.text:
The Powermate 2x consists of two parts:

The optical element

The removable eyepiece holder

To prepare the Powermate for use with the ASI174MM via T2 threads, unscrew the eyepiece holder and replace it with the T2 adapter ring.

If the T2 adapter doesn’t seem to fit at first, check its orientation. The correct side to thread in is the one where, when mounted, the printed text on both parts is not upside down relative to each other.

Once attached, the Powermate is ready for secure, threaded connection to the camera.

This is the configuration of the Powermate 2x used in the next two use cases.

The Powermate 2x (long cylinder) And The T2 Adapter Ring The Powermate 2x With Eyepiece Holder Unscrewed The Powermate 2x With T2 Adapter Ring Screwed In

docs.title: H-Alpha Full Disk Imaging
docs.text:
Mount the ASI174MM directly to the B1200 blocking filter diagonal using the T2 thread connection.

No additional adapters are needed. This configuration provides a full-disk solar image (depending on seeing and camera spacing) and uses the most stable mechanical interface.

Mounting the camera on B1200 blocking diagonal using T2 threads The camera mounted

docs.title: H-Alpha Imaging with Powermate 2x
docs.text:
Insert the Powermate 2x into the B1200 diagonal as usual.

Attach the T2 adapter to the Powermate and thread the ASI174MM directly onto it.

This provides a magnified view of solar surface details. As with the previous configuration, the entire camera train remains fully threaded for stability.

docs.title: White Light Full Disk Imaging
docs.text:
Insert the T2-to-1.25" eyepiece adapter into the Lunt Herschel wedge.

Mount the ASI174MM into the adapter and secure it using the thumb screw.

This is the only configuration that requires using the eyepiece-style connection, as the Herschel wedge does not offer T2 threads. Ensure the camera is seated firmly and the thumb screw is tight before imaging.

Inserting Camera In The Lunt Herschel Wedge Using The T2 - 1.2" Adapter

docs.title: White Light With Powermate 2x
docs.text:
Insert the Powermate 2x into the Lunt Herschel wedge.

Attach the T2 adapter to the Powermate and thread the ASI174MM to it.

The connection between the Herschel wedge and Powermate is eyepiece-style, but the camera and optical train after that remain fully threaded.

docs.title: Finding Sun With The SolarQuest
docs.text:
Once the telescope is mounted and ready for solar observation, the final step is to power on the SolarQuest mount and wait for it to automatically locate and track the Sun.

The video shows the full process. After powering on, the mount will first level the scope and return it to a known starting position. There will then be a brief pause where nothing appears to happen — this is normal. The mount is acquiring a GPS signal before continuing with solar alignment.

Your browser does not support the video tag. SolarQuest

docs.title: Inserting Equipment In The B1200 diagonal
docs.text:
When using the B1200 blocking filter diagonal, always angle it to the right side of the telescope (as seen from the rear). This ensures that gravity helps keep any inserted equipment seated in the diagonal, rather than applying torque that could loosen the diagonal from its threaded 2" base.

Angling the diagonal to the left may cause gravity to assist in unthreading the diagonal from its 2" base, especially when using heavier accessories. This has specifically occurred with the Pentax Zoom, due to its weight and leverage, but the principle applies more generally.

This is not a problem with the equipment itself — the Pentax Zoom is safe to use — but correct diagonal orientation prevents unintended loosening during use.

B1200 Blocking Filter Correctly Angled

docs.title: Install The B1200 Blocking Filter
docs.text:
Mount the B1200 blocking filter diagonal into the focuser as shown in the image. This is the only diagonal that can and must be used in this configuration to safely observe or image the Sun.

It is recommended to mount the telescope in this state first, and attach any viewing or imaging elements (Pentax Zoom, Powermate 2x, or camera in any combination) once the instrument is on the mount.

Ensure the thumb screws securing the diagonal are tightened before proceeding mounting the telescope.

LS60MT Configured For H-Alpha

docs.title: Install The Etalon Module
docs.text:
Mount the Etalon module in place of the focuser, as shown in the image. The plate with the serial number and Lunt cactus logo should align with the Tele Vue solar finder. Ensure all three thumb screws securing the Etalon module are tightened before proceeding.

LS60MT With Etalon Module Installed

docs.title: Mounting the Dovetail: Positioning
docs.text:
When attaching the telescope to the mount, the dovetail should be inserted almost all the way forward in the saddle track — close to the front stop, as shown in the image.

This forward mounting position helps maintain stable balance in the altitude (vertical) axis, particularly on alt-azimuth mounts.

Even with minimal equipment at the rear, this configuration is stable. If mounted near the center of the dovetail, heavier accessories — such as the 0.5 kg Pentax Zoom — can cause the telescope to tilt backward unexpectedly, resulting in unwanted altitude motion or tracking instability.

Forward positioning avoids imbalance and ensures smoother operation.

Correct Mounting

docs.title: Placing The Tripod With The SolarQuest Mount Before Mounting Telescope
docs.text:
Position the tripod and SolarQuest mount at the intended observing location before mounting the telescope. There is no need to spend time on precise leveling; the mount reliably tracks the Sun even with moderate unevenness. Field experience confirms stable tracking from morning to evening without adjustment.

Do not aim the mount toward the Sun. Leave it pointed in a safe, arbitrary direction. This ensures that the dovetail saddle is not facing the Sun during mounting, reducing the risk of looking into sunlight during alignment. The SolarQuest mount performs automatic solar alignment after power-on, handling Sun acquisition safely and independently.

SolarQuest mount on tripod not facing sun

docs.title: Preparing The Mount Saddle
docs.text:
Before mounting the telescope, ensure that the locking knob on the mount saddle is fully disengaged from the dovetail slot. After dismounting the telescope, the locking knob is often left partially threaded into the slot. This can obstruct the dovetail bar when sliding the telescope back into position.

Although it is possible to clear the obstruction while holding the telescope, it is safer and smoother to confirm that the saddle track is fully clear before mounting. This practice helps avoid fumbling and promotes good handling habits, especially as equipment size and weight increase.

Locking knob blocking smooth mounting of telescope

docs.title: Reinstall The Focuser
docs.text:
Re-mount the focuser onto the Etalon module as shown in the image. To ensure the focuser knobs are in a practical position, align them parallel with the Etalon’s pressure tuning knob. Ensure all three thumb screws securing the focuser are tightened before proceeding.

LS60MT With Etanol Modul And Focuser

docs.title: Remove The focuser
docs.text:
Loosen the tumb screws holding the focuser in place and remove the focuser, image shows the LS60MT with the focuser removed.

LS60MT Telescope Only

docs.title: Use of the Tele Vue Solar Finder
docs.text:
The Tele Vue Solar Finder is not strictly required when using the SolarQuest mount, as the mount reliably locates and tracks the Sun automatically. However, the finder is useful as a quick visual confirmation that the telescope is properly aligned.

When returning to the scope after a break, the pinhole projection provides immediate confirmation that the Sun is still centered. There have been cases where tracking was lost — for example, due to dead batteries or clouds blocking the Sun during initial alignment. Even if the sky is clear upon return, the mount may not have completed correction if cloud cover was present earlier.

The solar finder is especially helpful during imaging sessions: if nothing appears on the screen, but the Sun is visible through the pinhole, the scope is correctly aimed and the issue lies elsewhere (e.g., exposure settings, focus, or camera connection).

Sun In View

docs.title: Starting Configuration
docs.text:
The image shows LS60MT with out any solar filter (night use), this topic shows how to configure for H-Alpha observation and imagining (installing H-Alpha filters).

LS60MT With no solar filters mounted

docs.title: Using The 8-24 mm Pentax Zoom
docs.text:
When using the 8–24 mm Pentax Zoom for solar observation, it should be set to its widest setting (24 mm) before mounting.

This ensures:

The sharpest possible view under current seeing conditions (as a reliable starting point for further zooming)

The Sun is visible in the field of view, even if slightly off-center due to mount memory or Etalon tuning offset

Starting at minimum magnification helps avoid losing the Sun during initial viewing, especially after imaging or tuning adjustments.

The zoom at widest setting (24 mm)

docs.title: LS60MT Telescope Configurations
docs.text:
The LS60MT is primarily used to observe and image the Sun in the H-Alpha spectrum. It can also be configured for white light solar observation by removing the H-Alpha filters and installing the Lunt Herschel Diagonal, or used as a regular nighttime telescope with the William Optics Diagonal.

This topic provides an overview of the different configurations. It is particularly important for safety to understand when the LS60MT is properly configured for safe solar observation and imaging.

docs.title: LS60MT H-Alpha Solar Configuration
docs.text:
The LS60MT configured for observing and imaging the Sun in the H-Alpha spectrum. Be sure to read the description below the image.

LS60MT Ready For H-Alpha Solar Observing / Imaging

Two filters must be installed for safe use: the B1200 blocking filter and the pressure-tuned etalon filter. Both are easy to recognize:

  • The B1200 blocking filter is located inside the orange and white diagonal marked with the USA flag.
  • The etalon filter is the red module with the "LUNT SOLAR SYSTEM" plate and the large pressure-tuning knob.

docs.title: LS60MT Night Configuration
docs.text:
The LS60MT configured for use as a regular nighttime telescope. Be sure to read the description below the image.

Never point at the Sun

In this configuration no solar filters are mounted. The telescope must never be pointed at the Sun.

LS60MT Ready For Night Observing / Imaging
  • No solar filters are mounted. The telescope must not be used for any solar work.
  • The William Optics Diagonal is mounted with its included 2-inch to 1.25-inch reducer, allowing use of the Pentax Zoom. The same reducer is also used for mounting the Lunt Herschel Diagonal in the white light configuration.

docs.title: LS60MT White Light Solar Configuration
docs.text:
The LS60MT configured for observing and imaging the Sun in white light. Be sure to read the description below the picture.

LS60MT Ready For White Light Solar Observing / Imaging
  • The etalon filter module has been removed.
  • Safe solar observation is provided by the Lunt Herschel Diagonal, identified by its black housing, Lunt cactus logo, and orange warning plate indicating a hot area.
  • A 2-inch to 1.25-inch reducer is used to attach the diagonal to the focuser.
  • The Baader Single Polarizing Filter should be used with the Pentax Zoom for visual observing to reduce brightness. It is not dangerous to observe without it, but the light is too bright for comfortable viewing.

docs.title: Starting Configuration
docs.text:
The image shows the LS60MT configured for H-Alpha observation, with the etalon module and the blocking filter installed. This topic shows how to go from this configuration to the night observing configuration.

LS60MT Ready For H-Alpha Solar Observing / Imaging

docs.title: Mount the Lunt Herschel Wedge
docs.text:
To mount the 1.25" Lunt Herschel wedge to the 2" focuser, first insert the 2" to 1.25" reducer into the focuser.

Use the reducer normally found on the William Optics diagonal. It should still be attached to the diagonal or located with it.

Once the reducer is in place, insert the Herschel wedge and secure it with the thumb screw.

Note: With the reducer removed, the WO diagonal is left without a 2" dust cap. The orange 2" cap from the focuser can be used to protect the WO diagonal during this configuration.

Once the wedge is installed, it is best to mount the telescope on the tripod before attaching any viewing or imaging equipment. This applies especially to heavier accessories such as the Pentax Zoom, which should always be mounted when the scope is securely in place.

docs.title: Mount the Pentax Zoom Eyepiece
docs.text:
Insert the Pentax Zoom eyepiece into the Herschel wedge and secure it with the thumb screw.

Before mounting, screw the Baader single polarizing filter into the bottom of the eyepiece. This significantly reduces glare during visual observation.

To use the filter effectively, keep the thumb screw slightly loose so the eyepiece can be rotated. Slowly turn the eyepiece while viewing until the image appears darkest — this is the point of maximum glare reduction.

The Baader filter is a single polarizing filter, but it works in this configuration because the Herschel wedge itself acts as a polarizing element. Together, they allow effective light suppression through alignment of polarization axes.

The Pentax Zoom With Baader Single Polarizing Filter The Pentax Zoom With The Badder Single Polarizing Filter Screwed In

docs.title: Reattach the Focuser
docs.text:
Mount the focuser directly onto the telescope and secure it using the three thumb screws.

Ensure all screws are properly tightened before proceeding.

Focuser Mounted On Scope

docs.title: Remove The B1200 Blocking Filter Diagonal
docs.text:
Loosen the thumb screw on the focuser and carefully remove the B1200 blocking filter diagonal.

After removal, attach the appropriate dust covers to both the diagonal and the focuser to protect internal surfaces during the reconfiguration.

LS60MT With The B1200 Diagonal Removed

docs.title: Remove The Etalon Module
docs.text:
Loosen the three thumb screws securing the Etalon module to the telescope and carefully remove it.

Attach the front dust cap to protect the optical surface.

Store the Etalon module and the B1200 blocking filter in their designated storage locations.

Etalon Module Removed

docs.title: Remove Focuser From The Etalon Module
docs.text:
Loosen the three thumb screws securing the focuser to the rear of the Etalon module, and remove the focuser.

Attach the flat dust cap to the rear of the Etalon using the same thumb screws. This protects the internal surfaces during storage or when the Etalon is not in use.

Focuser Removed

docs.title: Starting Configuration
docs.text:
The image shows LS60MT configured for H-Alpha observation: With the etalon module and the blocking filter installed. This topic show how to go from this configuration to the white light solar observing configuration.

LS60MT Ready For H-Alpha Solar Observing / Imaging
